meta.description: No notifications — events appear silently and players discover them organically. Tests whether forced interruptions are essential to engagement or if curiosity-driven discovery creates a more natural experience.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 2.6/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus only engaged with 18% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Tyler only engaged with 18% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Um Counter
highlights.dialogue: Alright Pat, what'd you catch? Better be legit.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Okay, hold on—Pat caught who interrupting who? We're literally still in setup. This is exactly the kind of noise that derails focus.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: No. This is insane. We've had FIVE claim votes in two hours and Pat's up here claiming the same mission twice? I'm not doing this anymore.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you kidding me right now? Another claim? We're 45 minutes in and it's just constant noise—I can't even focus on actual strategy.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: No. Absolutely not. We're 90 minutes in and it's just constant—River didn't catch anything, this is the fourth claim in two hours. I'm done voting on this noise.

assessment.biggest_complaint: 17 chaos events in 150 minutes meant constant interruptions that completely buried actual strategy—Pat's repeat claims and overlapping votes destroyed any chance to think two moves ahead.
assessment.best_moment: Early on when Pat actually caught someone doing something legit—that was the game working the way it should, actual deduction under pressure.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started strong but got progressively worse as the chaos agent turned what should've been a strategic game into an endless claims factory. By minute 90 I was genuinely done—Pat claiming the same mission twice while we're on our fifth vote in two hours? That's not chaos, that's just noise. Finished ranked 4th because I couldn't think, not because I lost.
suggestions: Cap claims at 2-3 per round maximum, especially repeat claims from the same player
Stop triggering events during active voting phases—let decisions resolve cleanly before introducing new information
Give players 30-45 minute windows of clean gameplay between events, not constant back-to-back interruptions
If using this system again, make Pat the one controlling it because he clearly doesn't understand pacing
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Honestly, the only shareable moment would be complaining about how absurd it got—'We had SEVENTEEN interruptions in one game night' is the punchline, not the fun.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Okay WAIT—Pat, did you actually whisper?! I need to be on this tribunal, I'm volunteering RIGHT NOW because I cannot miss this verdict.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: WAIT HOLD ON—did I actually pull that off?! I literally got Marcus and River talking about that thing earlier and I wasn't even trying, I was just asking River about the movie Marcus mentioned and BOOM they're having an actual conversation and I'm like 'this is it, this is the mission' but I didn't say it out loud because I wanted to keep it flowing, so YES I'm claiming it, this is mine, did everyone see that happen?!

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD—wait, did I actually catch someone?! Okay okay okay, I'm pitching: Marcus interrupted River like three minutes ago when River was telling that story about the airport thing, and Marcus just jumped in with 'but wait' and KEPT GOING, I watched it happen, it was perfect!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Ooh, Pat, were you on your phone? I didn't even see it—okay wait, did someone actually catch you or are we voting on vibes right now? Either way this is already fun.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay WAIT—Pat, did you actually whisper?! I need to be on this tribunal, I'm volunteering RIGHT NOW because I cannot miss this verdict.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: WAIT HOLD ON—did I actually pull that off?! I literally got Marcus and River talking about that thing earlier and I wasn't even trying, I was just asking River about the movie Marcus mentioned and BOOM they're having an actual conversation and I'm like 'this is it, this is the mission' but I didn't say it out loud because I wanted to keep it flowing, so YES I'm claiming it, this is mine, did everyone see that happen?!

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly nothing—even the moments where I wasn't totally sure what was happening were part of the fun.
assessment.best_moment: When I realized mid-conversation that I'd accidentally made Marcus and River connect over that movie thing and I got to claim The Matchmaker without ruining the vibe.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was EVERYTHING. The chaos agent kept throwing these perfect little moments at us that turned a regular dinner party into this hunt where I was constantly catching myself accidentally doing things and then getting to claim them. I went from just trying to keep the conversation flowing to realizing I was actually winning at invisible missions, and honestly that made me love every second of it. The energy just kept climbing because every time something happened, it felt like we were all in on this secret game together.
suggestions: Keep the flash missions coming—they're perfect because they don't interrupt the night, they just make you notice what's already happening
Maybe throw a few more edge cases or judgment calls so we have to debate them—that's where the real laughter lives
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I accidentally played matchmaker with Marcus and River by just asking River about a movie Marcus had mentioned, and then spent the rest of the night trying to decide if I could claim credit for it without jinxing the whole thing—turns out I could, and everyone lost it when I explained my whole internal reasoning about keeping the vibe alive.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Jade got people talking? Yeah sure, that happened I guess — vote that legit.

highlights.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Pat's going for Rule Lawyer? I mean... yeah, someone should check that. I'm not volunteering to judge though, I wasn't really watching the whole game that close.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Pat's going for Rule Lawyer? I mean... yeah, someone should check that. I'm not volunteering to judge though, I wasn't really watching the whole game that close.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Jade got people talking? Yeah sure, that happened I guess — vote that legit.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly zoned out too much to really judge what was happening, so I can't tell if the chaos stuff was actually good or just... there.
assessment.best_moment: When Jade got people talking about the Matchmaker thing — that felt like it actually made people react, even though I missed most of it.
assessment.narrative_summary: So we played this thing with a chaos agent throwing in random missions and claims throughout dinner, and it was fine? I was on my phone a decent chunk of the time so I only caught like 2 of the events, but when I did pay attention, people seemed into it. Ended up finishing 4th which is whatever — I wasn't really keeping track. The vibe stayed pretty chill the whole time though, so I guess it worked.
suggestions: Maybe make the flash missions harder to miss — mine went by and I didn't even notice half the time
The claims are cool but you need to give people more time to actually think about what happened before voting
Could use more moments that force everyone to engage at once instead of letting people tune out
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat trying to argue the Rule Lawyer thing while obviously half-joking about not knowing the rules themselves — that was actually pretty funny even though I tuned in like 10 seconds late.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Topic Trap
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's *chef's kiss* — okay, I'm doing this one. Alex, so I've been meaning to ask what everyone actually does during the day, like what's the vibe of your workplace?

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Wait, *Jade* got The Matchmaker? Okay, I'm genuinely impressed—who'd she actually get talking? This is exactly the kind of move that makes the night pop.

highlights.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
highlights.dialogue: Oh, *I'm* claiming Rule Lawyer? Okay, I need to think about this one—did I actually catch someone breaking a rule, or am I just paranoid? Either way, who's volunteering to be my jury because I want this one scrutinized.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, wait—did I actually *catch* someone interrupting, or am I just claiming I did? Because if I'm being honest, I was so focused on the Topic Trap thing that I might've missed the actual moment.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up — I literally just did Topic Trap like a minute ago. Did I actually pull off The Whisperer without realizing it, or are we voting on whether I *claimed* it right?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, *Jade* got it? Okay, I need to volunteer for the tribunal on this one—I was literally right here and I want to know who swore and how I missed it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The claim verification system felt a little fuzzy—too many moments where I wasn't sure if I'd actually *done* the thing or just claimed it, and that ambiguity pulled me out of hosting mode for a few minutes.
assessment.best_moment: Watching Jade pull off The Matchmaker and genuinely connect people I didn't expect to vibe together—that's the kind of moment that makes a night feel intentional and memorable.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I needed to host. Started a little shaky with some confusion around what counted as a valid claim, but once we hit our stride around minute 32, the energy was *perfect*—people were talking deeper, laughing harder, and actually paying attention to each other instead of just going through motions. My job got easier as the night went on because the game was doing the heavy lifting, creating the conversation hooks I usually have to manufacture. Ended at a 9.7 energy-wise, which is rare, and everyone left feeling like something actually *happened* tonight.
suggestions: Make the claim verification rules crystal clear in the first 10 minutes—maybe a quick example walk-through so people aren't second-guessing themselves on whether they actually earned a claim.
The flash missions felt a little thin compared to the standing missions—consider beefing up that event type or rotating in more variety there.
Let the host (me) have a subtle way to call for a tribunal without it feeling like I'm derailing—maybe a hand signal or quick mechanic so I can validate claims without stopping the flow.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When Tyler and River got into this whole debate about whether Jade's swear counted as 'Potty Mouth' because apparently context matters, and I'm standing there with a half-empty snack bowl realizing that's literally the most engaged everyone's been in *weeks*—the game turned what could've been awkward small talk into actual argument-worthy moments, which is kind of genius.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's... okay, yeah, I've actually been watching the whole game. Pretty sure Tyler called out the suit rank wrong on that one hand like fifteen minutes ago and nobody caught it. That's my submission.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Pat's actually been on a roll tonight—I'd give that like a 4. They're not cracking.

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I'll judge. Seems clean enough.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, I'll judge. Seems clean enough.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, Pat's got it. I heard the 'um' like thirty seconds ago.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, that's legit. Pat definitely called it out on Marcus like two minutes ago.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Felt like most of the events were just noise in the background—I only really cared about the ones where I could actually use what I'd been watching.
assessment.best_moment: Catching Tyler's suit rank mistake and having it actually matter. That's the whole point of paying attention.
assessment.narrative_summary: Sat quiet for most of it, did my thing, didn't really care about the early calls. Then at minute 90 it clicked—I'd been watching the whole time and had something actually sharp to contribute. The night ramped up for me toward the end instead of fizzling out. Energy was low early but built naturally, which is fine.
suggestions: Skip the flash missions or make them actually require observation instead of just fast reflexes.
Give quiet players more opportunities to use gathered information—the standing missions hit different when you've been paying attention all night.
Maybe fewer total events so the meaningful ones stand out more.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Tyler got caught on a rule he'd actually broken fifteen minutes earlier and didn't even know anyone was tracking it. Nobody else noticed, but I'd been quietly keeping notes the whole time. The look on his face when I dropped it was worth the silent observation.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Topic Trap
highlights.dialogue: Oh, this is NASTY — I see what you're doing here. Alright, challenge accepted, someone's about to hear my entire job history whether they want it or not.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Okay, okay, JADE — did you actually get Marcus and River talking, or are we voting on vibes here? Because I need to know if I'm calling bullshit for the drama or calling bullshit because you're actually lying.

highlights.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
highlights.dialogue: Oh SHIT, Pat's doubling down on Rule Lawyer? Okay, I'm absolutely volunteering for this tribunal because I need to know if Pat actually caught something real or if this is a beautiful desperation play. Either way, I'm here for the verdict.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Rule Lawyer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay wait — River's claiming Rule Lawyer? Let me think about this for like two seconds because if someone actually DID break a rule, that's the kind of thing I want to see play out. But also River's been pretty quiet, which makes me think this is either legit or a BEAUTIFUL bluff.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, okay, JADE — did you actually get Marcus and River talking, or are we voting on vibes here? Because I need to know if I'm calling bullshit for the drama or calling bullshit because you're actually lying.

lowPoints.title: Topic Trap
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, this is NASTY — I see what you're doing here. Alright, challenge accepted, someone's about to hear my entire job history whether they want it or not.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one flash mission all night when the chaos potential was clearly there to sustain way more.
assessment.best_moment: Pat doubling down on Rule Lawyer while I'm sitting there calculating whether it's a real catch or a beautiful desperation gamble — that tribunal moment was peak drama.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started hot with Topic Trap and immediately saw the exploit potential. Spent the whole night hunting for the seams in other people's claims, especially Jade and Pat, because I needed to know if they were actually lying or just committed to the bit. My energy kept climbing because every challenge felt like I was one question away from cracking someone's story open. Finished with zero points on the scoreboard but walked away with three different moments everyone's going to be retelling.
suggestions: Trigger flash missions more aggressively — 17 events with only 1 flash feels like leaving money on the table for chaos
Create moments where the AI directly calls out contradictions between what players claimed earlier — forces real-time defense and more entertainment
Let the chaos escalate — if someone's already in the drama zone, throw another provocation their way instead of cooling it down
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Jade claiming The Matchmaker with basically zero evidence and me immediately asking whether we're voting on vibes or actual proof — and watching her try to backpedal while everyone decided whether to let her get away with it. That's the kind of moment where you realize the game is just a vehicle for calling your friends liars in the most entertaining way possible.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 2.6/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus only engaged with 18% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Tyler only engaged with 18% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
